DEMO: Linear Variational Method

What You Need to Know

  • The variational method is a powerful tool for estimating upper bounds and approximations for ground-state energies in a wide range of quantum mechanical problems.

  • It plays a key role in electronic structure theories, such as Hartree-Fock.

  • The linear variational method seeks solutions to the Schrödinger equation by representing trial wavefunctions as linear combinations of simple, computationally efficient functions, such as Gaussians or exponentials.

  • By applying the linear variational method, the Schrödinger equation is transformed into a linear algebra problem, where the goal is to find eigenvalues (representing energies) and eigenvectors (providing coefficients for the linear combination).

Linearizing the problem¶

Basis sets and coefficients

  • How does linearization help us in practice? If we can not exactly solve and have an expression for ψ\psiψ then we can expand the wavefunction in some convenient basis set fnf_nfn​ (e.g bunch of functions indexed by n, like fourier series) and attempt to make it get as closer to realistic energies and wavefunction as possible.

ϕ(r)=∑nNcnfn(r)\phi(r) = \sum_n^N c_nf_n(r)ϕ(r)=n∑N​cn​fn​(r)
(1)

  • If we have infinite number of basis functions in pricniple that should approximate any funciton and we can get nearly exact numerical solution. However computationally this is not feasible.

  • Truncating this expansion at any finite nnn leads to an approximate solution. The idea is to keep just the optimal number of basis functions.

  • The variational principle tells us is that we can minimize the energy with respect to variational paramters cn{c_n}cn​ and still have Eϕ≥E0E_\phi \geq E_0Eϕ​≥E0​.

Gaussians are commonly used as basis sets

  • Gaussian-type orbitals (GTOs) are a standard example because they make integrals analytically tractable. Consider a simple 1s-like primitive Gaussian:

f(r)=e−αr2f(r) = e^{-\alpha r^2}f(r)=e−αr2
(2)

  • To represent a more flexible radial shape, we expand the true orbital as a linear combination of Gaussians:

ϕ(r)=∑n=1Ncne−αnr2\phi(r) = \sum_{n=1}^N c_n e^{-\alpha_n r^2}ϕ(r)=n=1∑N​cn​e−αn​r2
(3)

  • Even though a hydrogenic 1s orbital behaves as e−r/a0e^{-r/a_0}e−r/a0​, which is not Gaussian, we can approximate its shape remarkably well using only 3 to 6 Gaussians. This is the idea behind the STO-nG basis sets.

Smallest example¶

  • We will illustrate this idea and the general matrix construction with a simple example of two basis functions (N=2N=2N=2)

ϕ=c1f1+c2f2\phi = c_1f_1 + c_2f_2ϕ=c1​f1​+c2​f2​
(4)

  • There is currently no need to define these functions explicitly so we will leave them as generic functions f1f_1f1​ and f2f_2f2​. We now solve for the energy

Eϕ=⟨ϕ∣H^∣ϕ⟩⟨ϕ∣ϕ⟩=⟨c1f1+c2f2∣H^∣c1f1+c2f2⟩⟨c1f1+c2f2∣c1f1+c2f2⟩E_\phi = \frac{\langle\phi|\hat{H}|\phi\rangle}{\langle\phi|\phi\rangle} = \frac{\langle c_1f_1 + c_2f_2|\hat{H}|c_1f_1 + c_2f_2 \rangle}{\langle c_1f_1 + c_2f_2|c_1f_1 + c_2f_2 \rangle}Eϕ​=⟨ϕ∣ϕ⟩⟨ϕ∣H^∣ϕ⟩​=⟨c1​f1​+c2​f2​∣c1​f1​+c2​f2​⟩⟨c1​f1​+c2​f2​∣H^∣c1​f1​+c2​f2​⟩​
(5)
Express problem in terms of coefficients and matrix elements

  • Let’s investigate the numerator and denomenator separately. We start with the numerator

⟨c1f1+c2f2∣H^∣c1f1+c2f2⟩=⟨c1f1∣H^∣c1f1⟩+⟨c1f1∣H^∣c2f2⟩+⟨c2f2∣H^∣c1f1⟩+⟨c2f2∣H^∣c2f2⟩\langle c_1f_1 + c_2f_2|\hat{H}|c_1f_1 + c_2f_2 \rangle = \langle c_1f_1|\hat{H}|c_1f_1\rangle + \langle c_1f_1|\hat{H}|c_2f_2\rangle +\langle c_2f_2|\hat{H}|c_1f_1\rangle + \langle c_2f_2|\hat{H}|c_2f_2\rangle⟨c1​f1​+c2​f2​∣H^∣c1​f1​+c2​f2​⟩=⟨c1​f1​∣H^∣c1​f1​⟩+⟨c1​f1​∣H^∣c2​f2​⟩+⟨c2​f2​∣H^∣c1​f1​⟩+⟨c2​f2​∣H^∣c2​f2​⟩
(6)
=c1∗c1⟨f1∣H^∣f1⟩+c1∗c2⟨f1∣H^∣f2⟩+c2∗c1⟨f2∣H^∣f1⟩+c2∗c2⟨f2∣H^∣f2⟩= c_1^*c_1\langle f_1|\hat{H}|f_1\rangle + c_1^*c_2\langle f_1|\hat{H}|f_2\rangle +c_2^*c_1\langle f_2|\hat{H}|f_1\rangle + c_2^*c_2\langle f_2|\hat{H}|f_2\rangle=c1∗​c1​⟨f1​∣H^∣f1​⟩+c1∗​c2​⟨f1​∣H^∣f2​⟩+c2∗​c1​⟨f2​∣H^∣f1​⟩+c2∗​c2​⟨f2​∣H^∣f2​⟩
(7)

  • We note that cic_ici​ are real and thus c1∗c1=c12c_1^*c_1=c_1^2c1∗​c1​=c12​ and c1c2=c2c1c_1c_2=c_2c_1c1​c2​=c2​c1​ thus

Numerator=c12⟨f1∣H^∣f1⟩+c1c2⟨f1∣H^∣f2⟩+c2c1⟨f2∣H^∣f1⟩+c22⟨f2∣H^∣f2⟩Numerator = c_1^2 \langle f_1|\hat{H}|f_1\rangle + c_1c_2\langle f_1|\hat{H}|f_2\rangle +c_2c_1\langle f_2|\hat{H}|f_1\rangle + c_2^2\langle f_2|\hat{H}|f_2\rangleNumerator=c12​⟨f1​∣H^∣f1​⟩+c1​c2​⟨f1​∣H^∣f2​⟩+c2​c1​⟨f2​∣H^∣f1​⟩+c22​⟨f2​∣H^∣f2​⟩
(8)

  • Finally we recognize that since H^\hat{H}H^ is Hermitian, we have ⟨f2∣H^∣f1⟩=⟨f1∣H^∣f2⟩\langle f_2|\hat{H}|f_1\rangle = \langle f_1|\hat{H}|f_2\rangle⟨f2​∣H^∣f1​⟩=⟨f1​∣H^∣f2​⟩ yielding

Numerator=c12⟨f1∣H^∣f1⟩+2c1c2⟨f1∣H^∣f2⟩+c22⟨f2∣H^∣f2⟩Numerator = c_1^2 \langle f_1|\hat{H}|f_1\rangle + 2c_1c_2\langle f_1|\hat{H}|f_2\rangle + c_2^2\langle f_2|\hat{H}|f_2\rangleNumerator=c12​⟨f1​∣H^∣f1​⟩+2c1​c2​⟨f1​∣H^∣f2​⟩+c22​⟨f2​∣H^∣f2​⟩
(9)

  • We will refer to the integrals above as matrix elements with, generically, Hij=⟨fi∣H^∣fj⟩H_{ij} = \langle f_i|\hat{H}|f_j\rangleHij​=⟨fi​∣H^∣fj​⟩. We now consider the denominator of the energy equation above and we utilize similar algebra for the numerator to get

⟨c1f1+c2f2∣c1f1+c2f2⟩=c12S11+2c1c2S12+c22S22\langle c_1f_1 + c_2f_2|c_1f_1 + c_2f_2 \rangle = c_1^2S_{11} + 2c_1c_2S_{12} + c_2^2S_{22}⟨c1​f1​+c2​f2​∣c1​f1​+c2​f2​⟩=c12​S11​+2c1​c2​S12​+c22​S22​
(10)
Sij=⟨fi∣fj⟩S_{ij} = \langle f_i|f_j\rangleSij​=⟨fi​∣fj​⟩
(11)

where SijS_{ij}Sij​ is another matrix element but this time referred to as the basis function overlap. Now we have that the energy of the trial wavefunction is

Eϕ=c12H11+2c1c2H12+c22H22c12S11+2c1c2S12+c22S22E_\phi = \frac{c_1^2 H_{11} + 2c_1c_2H_{12} + c_2^2H_{22}}{c_1^2S_{11} + 2c_1c_2S_{12} + c_2^2S_{22}}Eϕ​=c12​S11​+2c1​c2​S12​+c22​S22​c12​H11​+2c1​c2​H12​+c22​H22​​
(12)

  • Hij=⟨fi∣H^∣fj⟩H_{ij} = \langle f_i |\hat{H}|f_j\rangleHij​=⟨fi​∣H^∣fj​⟩ Hamiltonian matrix element expressed in basis of fff

  • Sij=⟨fi∣fj⟩S_{ij} = \langle f_i|f_j\rangleSij​=⟨fi​∣fj​⟩ S matrix element or overlap integral expressed in basis of fff. This one measures how similiar fif_ifi​ is to fjf_jfj​.

Eigenvalue Problem¶

  • Since Eϕ≥E0E_\phi \geq E_0Eϕ​≥E0​ for any trial function ϕ\phiϕ, we can minimize the energy EϕE_\phiEϕ​ by varying the parameters c1c_1c1​ and c2c_2c2​.

  • To minimize with respect to c1c_1c1​, we differentiate EϕE_\phiEϕ​ with respect to c1c_1c1​ and set the derivative equal to zero:

    ∂Eϕ∂c1=0=c1(H11−ES11)+c2(H12−ES12)\frac{\partial E_\phi}{\partial c_1} = 0 = c_1(H_{11} - ES_{11}) + c_2(H_{12} - ES_{12})∂c1​∂Eϕ​​=0=c1​(H11​−ES11​)+c2​(H12​−ES12​)
    (13)

  • Similarly, minimizing with respect to c2c_2c2​ gives:

    ∂Eϕ∂c2=0=c1(H12−ES12)+c2(H22−ES22)\frac{\partial E_\phi}{\partial c_2} = 0 = c_1(H_{12} - ES_{12}) + c_2(H_{22} - ES_{22})∂c2​∂Eϕ​​=0=c1​(H12​−ES12​)+c2​(H22​−ES22​)
    (14)

  • These two coupled linear equations can be expressed compactly as a matrix equation:

    [H11−ES11H12−ES12H12−ES12H22−ES22][c1c2]=0\begin{bmatrix} H_{11} - ES_{11} & H_{12} - ES_{12} \\ H_{12} - ES_{12} & H_{22} - ES_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} c_1 \\ c_2 \end{bmatrix} = 0[H11​−ES11​H12​−ES12​​H12​−ES12​H22​−ES22​​][c1​c2​​]=0
    (15)

  • The matrix on the left can be rewritten as the difference between two matrices:

    ([H11H12H12H22]−E[S11S12S12S22])[c1c2]=0\left(\begin{bmatrix} H_{11} & H_{12} \\ H_{12} & H_{22} \end{bmatrix} - E \begin{bmatrix} S_{11} & S_{12} \\ S_{12} & S_{22} \end{bmatrix} \right) \begin{bmatrix} c_1 \\ c_2 \end{bmatrix} = 0([H11​H12​​H12​H22​​]−E[S11​S12​​S12​S22​​])[c1​c2​​]=0
    (16)

  • Rearranging, we can write:

    [H11H12H12H22][c1c2]=E[S11S12S12S22][c1c2]\begin{bmatrix} H_{11} & H_{12} \\ H_{12} & H_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} c_1 \\ c_2 \end{bmatrix} = E \begin{bmatrix} S_{11} & S_{12} \\ S_{12} & S_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} c_1 \\ c_2 \end{bmatrix}[H11​H12​​H12​H22​​][c1​c2​​]=E[S11​S12​​S12​S22​​][c1​c2​​]
    (17)

  • Moving S matrix to left hand side we end up with eigenvector eigenvalue problem:

[S11S12S12S22]−1[H11H12H12H22][c1c2]=E[1001][c1c2]\begin{bmatrix} S_{11} & S_{12} \\ S_{12} & S_{22} \end{bmatrix}^{-1}\begin{bmatrix} H_{11} & H_{12} \\ H_{12} & H_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} c_1 \\ c_2 \end{bmatrix} = E \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} c_1 \\ c_2 \end{bmatrix}[S11​S12​​S12​S22​​]−1[H11​H12​​H12​H22​​][c1​c2​​]=E[10​01​][c1​c2​​]
(18)
Reducing QM to a matrix eigenvalue eigenvector problem
Hc=ESc\mathbf{H}\mathbf{c} = E\mathbf{S}\mathbf{c}Hc=ESc
(19)

  • By left-multiplying both sides by S−1\mathbf{S}^{-1}S−1, we transform this into a standard eigenvalue problem:

    S−1H⋅c=EI⋅c\mathbf{S}^{-1}\mathbf{H}\cdot \mathbf{c} = E\mathbf{I} \cdot \mathbf{c}S−1H⋅c=EI⋅c
    (20)

  • Therefore, the minimum energies correspond to the eigenvalues of S−1H\mathbf{S}^{-1}\mathbf{H}S−1H, and the variational parameters that minimize the energies are the eigenvectors of S−1H\mathbf{S}^{-1}\mathbf{H}S−1H.

  • H\mathbf{H}H N by N matrix of hamiltonian elements ⟨fi∣H^∣fj⟩\langle f_i |\hat{H}|f_j\rangle⟨fi​∣H^∣fj​⟩

  • SSS an N by N matrix of overlap integrals ⟨fi∣fj⟩\langle f_i|f_j\rangle⟨fi​∣fj​⟩

  • c=(c1,c2,...)\mathbf{c} = (c_1, c_2, ...)c=(c1​,c2​,...) vector of N length.

  • EEE eigenvalues that can saitsfy this equation. For symmetric matrices one expects to get N possible values!

Solving a matrix eigenvalue eigenvector problem

In the equation

S−1Hc=EIc\mathbf{S}^{-1}\mathbf{H}\mathbf{c} = E\mathbf{I}\mathbf{c}S−1Hc=EIc
(21)

I\mathbf{I}I represents the identity matrix. Its role in this context is essential to express the equation as a standard eigenvalue problem.

  1. Eigenvalue Problem Form:

    In linear algebra, a standard eigenvalue problem is written as:

    Av=λIv\mathbf{A}\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{I}\mathbf{v}Av=λIv
    (22)

    where:

    • A\mathbf{A}A is a square matrix,

    • λ\lambdaλ is a scalar eigenvalue,

    • I\mathbf{I}I is the identity matrix, and

    • v\mathbf{v}v is the corresponding eigenvector.

    The identity matrix I\mathbf{I}I ensures that λ\lambdaλ scales the eigenvector v\mathbf{v}v without altering its direction. The eigenvalue problem is about finding the values of λ\lambdaλ and their associated v\mathbf{v}v.

  2. Connecting to S−1Hc=EIc\mathbf{S}^{-1}\mathbf{H}\mathbf{c} = E\mathbf{I}\mathbf{c}S−1Hc=EIc:

    Here, S−1H\mathbf{S}^{-1}\mathbf{H}S−1H acts as the operator A\mathbf{A}A in the standard eigenvalue problem.

    • S−1H\mathbf{S}^{-1}\mathbf{H}S−1H is a matrix resulting from left-multiplying H\mathbf{H}H by the inverse of S\mathbf{S}S.

    • c\mathbf{c}c represents the eigenvector.

    • EEE is the eigenvalue (corresponding to the energy in the quantum mechanical system).

    The identity matrix I\mathbf{I}I is explicitly included to highlight that EEE is a scalar multiplying the vector c\mathbf{c}c. This ensures that the left-hand side (a matrix operation) matches the right-hand side (a scaled vector).

  3. Why S−1\mathbf{S}^{-1}S−1 Appears:

    Initially, we had:

    Hc=ESc\mathbf{H}\mathbf{c} = E\mathbf{S}\mathbf{c}Hc=ESc
    (23)

    which cannot directly be interpreted as an eigenvalue problem because of the presence of S\mathbf{S}S (the overlap matrix). To transform this into a standard form, we pre-multiply both sides by S−1\mathbf{S}^{-1}S−1:

    S−1Hc=ES−1Sc\mathbf{S}^{-1}\mathbf{H}\mathbf{c} = E\mathbf{S}^{-1}\mathbf{S}\mathbf{c}S−1Hc=ES−1Sc
    (24)

    Since S−1S=I\mathbf{S}^{-1}\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{I}S−1S=I, this simplifies to:

    S−1Hc=EIc\mathbf{S}^{-1}\mathbf{H}\mathbf{c} = E\mathbf{I}\mathbf{c}S−1Hc=EIc
    (25)

  4. How to Interpret This as an Eigenvalue Problem:
    The equation now has the form of a standard eigenvalue problem:

    Av=λIv\mathbf{A}\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{I}\mathbf{v}Av=λIv
    (26)

    where:

    • A=S−1H\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{S}^{-1}\mathbf{H}A=S−1H is the effective matrix to diagonalize,

    • λ=E\lambda = Eλ=E are the eigenvalues, corresponding to the energy levels,

    • v=c\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{c}v=c are the eigenvectors, containing the coefficients of the trial wavefunctions.

  5. Physical Interpretation:
    Solving the eigenvalue problem S−1Hc=EIc\mathbf{S}^{-1}\mathbf{H}\mathbf{c} = E\mathbf{I}\mathbf{c}S−1Hc=EIc gives the approximate energies (EEE) of the quantum system as eigenvalues and the corresponding variational parameters (c\mathbf{c}c) as eigenvectors. The identity matrix I\mathbf{I}I is crucial for preserving the standard form of the eigenvalue problem, ensuring proper mathematical and physical interpretation.

Example: Particle in a Box¶

  • Let’s consider a free particle in 1D bounded to 0≤x≤a0\leq x\leq a0≤x≤a. The Hamiltonian for such a system is simply the kinetic energy operator

H^=−ℏ22md2dx2\hat{H} = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\frac{d^2}{dx^2}H^=−2mℏ2​dx2d2​
(27)

  • While we can have solved this problem analytically, it will be instructive to see how the variational solution works. We start by approximating ψ(x)\psi(x)ψ(x) as an expansion in two basis functions f1=x(a−x)f_1=x(a-x)f1​=x(a−x) and f2=x2(a−x)2f_2=x^2(a-x)^2f2​=x2(a−x)2

ψ(x)≈c1x(a−x)+c2x2(a−x)2\psi(x) \approx c_1x(a-x) + c_2x^2(a-x)^2ψ(x)≈c1​x(a−x)+c2​x2(a−x)2
(28)

  • The c1c_1c1​ and c2c_2c2​ are the variational parameters to find them we first construct the Hamiltonian matrix, H\mathbf{H}H, and the basis function overlap matrix, S\mathbf{S}S.

Hij=⟨fi∣H^∣fj⟩H_{ij} = \langle f_i|\hat{H}|f_j\rangleHij​=⟨fi​∣H^∣fj​⟩
(29)
Sij=⟨fi∣fj⟩S_{ij} = \langle f_i|f_j\rangleSij​=⟨fi​∣fj​⟩
(30)

  • We will then compute and diagonlize the S−1H\mathbf{S}^{-1}\mathbf{H}S−1H matrix.

Computing the matrix elements

In this problem, we have two basis functions

f1(x)=x(a−x)f_1(x) = x(a-x)f1​(x)=x(a−x)
(31)

and

f2(x)=x2(a−x)2f_2(x) = x^2(a-x)^2f2​(x)=x2(a−x)2
(32)

now the task of computing matrix elements comes down to forming integrals with these two and carrying out integration

H11=⟨x(a−x)∣H^∣x(a−x)⟩H_{11} = \langle x(a-x)|\hat{H}|x(a-x)\rangleH11​=⟨x(a−x)∣H^∣x(a−x)⟩
(33)
=⟨ax∣H^∣ax⟩−⟨ax∣H^∣x2⟩−⟨x2∣H^∣ax⟩+⟨x2∣H^∣x2⟩= \langle ax|\hat{H}|ax\rangle -\langle ax|\hat{H}|x^2\rangle - \langle x^2|\hat{H}|ax\rangle + \langle x^2|\hat{H}|x^2\rangle=⟨ax∣H^∣ax⟩−⟨ax∣H^∣x2⟩−⟨x2∣H^∣ax⟩+⟨x2∣H^∣x2⟩
(34)
=−a⟨x∣H^∣x2⟩+⟨x2∣H^∣x2⟩= -a\langle x|\hat{H}|x^2\rangle + \langle x^2|\hat{H}|x^2\rangle=−a⟨x∣H^∣x2⟩+⟨x2∣H^∣x2⟩
(35)

where the last equality holds because the second derivative of xxx with respect to x is zero. Let’s now investigate each of these integrals

⟨x∣H^∣x2⟩=∫0ax−ℏ22md2dx2x2dx=−ℏ2m∫0axdx=−ℏ2a22m\langle x|\hat{H}|x^2\rangle = \int_0^ax \frac{-\hbar^2}{2m}\frac{d^2}{dx^2} x^2dx \\ = \frac{-\hbar^2}{m}\int_0^axdx = \frac{-\hbar^2a^2}{2m}⟨x∣H^∣x2⟩=∫0a​x2m−ℏ2​dx2d2​x2dx=m−ℏ2​∫0a​xdx=2m−ℏ2a2​
(36)

Similarly we should get

⟨x2∣H^∣x2⟩=−ℏ2a33m\langle x^2|\hat{H}|x^2\rangle =\frac{-\hbar^2a^3}{3m}⟨x2∣H^∣x2⟩=3m−ℏ2a3​
(37)

Thus

H11=−a−ℏ2a22m+−ℏ2a33m=ℏ2a36mH_{11} = -a\frac{-\hbar^2a^2}{2m} + \frac{-\hbar^2a^3}{3m} = \frac{\hbar^2a^3}{6m}H11​=−a2m−ℏ2a2​+3m−ℏ2a3​=6mℏ2a3​
(38)
H22=⟨x2(a−x)2∣H^∣x2(a−x)2⟩H_{22} = \langle x^2(a-x)^2|\hat{H}|x^2(a-x)^2\rangleH22​=⟨x2(a−x)2∣H^∣x2(a−x)2⟩
(39)
=⟨a2x2∣H^∣a2x2⟩−2⟨a2x2∣H^∣2ax3⟩+⟨a2x2∣H^∣x4⟩−2⟨ax3∣H^∣a2x2⟩+4⟨ax3∣H^∣ax3⟩−2⟨ax3∣H^∣x4⟩+⟨x4∣H^∣a2x2⟩−2⟨x4∣H^∣ax3⟩+⟨x4∣H^∣x4⟩= \langle a^2x^2|\hat{H}|a^2x^2\rangle -2\langle a^2x^2|\hat{H}|2ax^3\rangle + \langle a^2x^2|\hat{H}|x^4\rangle -2\langle ax^3|\hat{H}|a^2x^2\rangle + 4\langle ax^3|\hat{H}|ax^3\rangle -2\langle ax^3|\hat{H}|x^4\rangle + \langle x^4|\hat{H}|a^2x^2\rangle - 2\langle x^4|\hat{H}|ax^3\rangle + \langle x^4|\hat{H}|x^4\rangle=⟨a2x2∣H^∣a2x2⟩−2⟨a2x2∣H^∣2ax3⟩+⟨a2x2∣H^∣x4⟩−2⟨ax3∣H^∣a2x2⟩+4⟨ax3∣H^∣ax3⟩−2⟨ax3∣H^∣x4⟩+⟨x4∣H^∣a2x2⟩−2⟨x4∣H^∣ax3⟩+⟨x4∣H^∣x4⟩
(40)
=−ℏ2a73m+3ℏ2a7m−6ℏ2a75m+ℏ2a72m−12ℏ2a75m+2ℏ2a7m−ℏ2a75m+ℏ2a7m−6ℏ2a77m= \frac{-\hbar^2a^7}{3m} +\frac{3\hbar^2 a^7}{m} - \frac{6\hbar^2 a^7}{5m} +\frac{\hbar^2 a^7}{2m} - \frac{12\hbar^2 a^7}{5m} +\frac{2\hbar^2 a^7}{m} - \frac{\hbar^2 a^7}{5m} + \frac{\hbar^2 a^7}{m} - \frac{6\hbar^2 a^7}{7m}=3m−ℏ2a7​+m3ℏ2a7​−5m6ℏ2a7​+2mℏ2a7​−5m12ℏ2a7​+m2ℏ2a7​−5mℏ2a7​+mℏ2a7​−7m6ℏ2a7​
(41)
=ℏ2a7105m= \frac{\hbar^2a^7}{105m}=105mℏ2a7​
(42)
H12=H21=ℏ2a530mH_{12} = H_{21} = \frac{\hbar^2a^5}{30m}H12​=H21​=30mℏ2a5​
(43)

We can now complete the Hamiltonian matrix, H\mathbf{H}H,

H=ℏ2a3m[16a230a230a4105]\mathbf{H} = \frac{\hbar^2a^3}{m} \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{6} & \frac{a^2}{30}\\ \frac{a^2}{30} & \frac{a^4}{105} \end{bmatrix}H=mℏ2a3​[61​30a2​​30a2​105a4​​]
(44)

Similarily one could carry out all the integrals for S\mathbf{S}S

S=a510[13a214a214a463]\mathbf{S} = \frac{a^5}{10} \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{3} & \frac{a^2}{14}\\ \frac{a^2}{14} & \frac{a^4}{63} \end{bmatrix}S=10a5​[31​14a2​​14a2​63a4​​]
(45)

  • We have obtaind all matrix elements in previous section and are now ready to obtain eigenvalues

H=ℏ2a3m[16a230a230a4105]\mathbf{H} = \frac{\hbar^2a^3}{m} \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{6} & \frac{a^2}{30}\\ \frac{a^2}{30} & \frac{a^4}{105} \end{bmatrix}H=mℏ2a3​[61​30a2​​30a2​105a4​​]
(46)
S=a510[13a214a214a463]\mathbf{S} = \frac{a^5}{10} \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{3} & \frac{a^2}{14}\\ \frac{a^2}{14} & \frac{a^4}{63} \end{bmatrix}S=10a5​[31​14a2​​14a2​63a4​​]
(47)
Source
import numpy as np
from scipy.linalg import eig, inv, eigh

# Lets adopt simpler units
a = 1.0
hbar = 1.0
m = 1.0

S = np.array([[1.0/30.0, 1.0/140.0],[1.0/140.0,1.0/630.0]])
H = np.array([[1.0/6.0,1.0/30.0],[1.0/30.0,1.0/105.0]])

e, v = eig(inv(S) @ H) #e, v = eigh(H, S) #Same values eigh is for hermitian matrices

print("Eigenvalues:", e)
print("First eigenvector:", v[:,0])
Eigenvalues: [ 4.93487481+0.j 51.06512519+0.j]
First eigenvector: [-0.66168489 -0.74978204]

So we see that the smallest energy in this basis is

Eϕ=4.9349ℏ2mE_\phi = 4.9349 \frac{\hbar^2}{m}Eϕ​=4.9349mℏ2​
(48)

How does this compare to the analytic solution? Plugging in for the ground state, n=1n=1n=1, and a=1a=1a=1 we get

E1=π2ℏ22≈4.9348ℏ2mE_1 = \frac{\pi^2\hbar^2}{2} \approx 4.9348 \frac{\hbar^2}{m}E1​=2π2ℏ2​≈4.9348mℏ2​
(49)

So we can see that our variational solution worked out well for the energy. Now how about the wavefunction?

Source
# plot wavefunction
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import integrate
%matplotlib inline
# x values
x = np.arange(0,1,0.01)
# exact wavefunction
psi1Exact = np.sqrt(2)*np.sin(np.pi*x) 
plt.plot(x,psi1Exact,'k-',label="Exact",lw=3)
# variational basis function wavefunction
psi1Var = v[0,0]*x*(1-x) + v[1,0]*x**2*(1-x)**2
norm = np.sqrt(integrate.simpson(np.power(psi1Var,2),x))
plt.plot(x,-psi1Var/norm,'r--', label="Variational")
plt.xlabel('x')
plt.ylabel('$P(x)$')
plt.legend()
